The 'give every dollar a job' philosophy. With Mint I'd look at spending after the fact and feel bad. YNAB has me decide before I spend. Total mindset shift.
Proactive allocation vs. reactive reporting. That's the core of zero-based budgeting. Makes sense it landed differently.

Avalanche wins on math, snowball wins on psychology. You ran the numbers and chose math — that's a valid preference if you can sustain it.

Good self-knowledge — the motivator matters more than the method. Avalanche is the right call for you.

Interesting — the higher stakes of the bigger loan creates accountability. Different psychological mechanism, same goal.

source_profile: early-career saver, opened separate savings account due to spending behavior

turns.content: I keep spending my savings. Every time I hit a goal amount I dip into it for something. Last month I had $2,000 saved and spent $400 of it on a concert trip.
Classic problem — savings in the same place as spending creates easy access. The friction to spend is zero. Separate institution is usually the fix.
I know I need to fix it. It's just too easy to transfer within my bank's app.
One tap is too frictionless for savings. Separate bank with a transfer delay is the structural solution.
I opened a savings account at Ally. No debit card, no ATM access, can't connect it to Venmo. Transfer takes two days.
Ally high-yield with friction-by-design — no debit card, no instant transfer. What triggered you to finally do it?
Honestly the concert trip embarrassed me enough. I worked two weeks for that $400 and spent it on one weekend.
The concert trip was the catalyst. Sometimes one clear moment of regret is all it takes to build the right structure.
